title: Looking at Cells Under a Microscope
subject: biology
context: A student uses a light microscope to observe a thin section of onion epidermis and a sample of human cheek cells. She makes labelled drawings of both cells and notes the differences between them. Her teacher asks her to compare these eukaryotic cells with a photograph of a bacterium.

plantCell.imageAlt: Diagram of a plant cell showing five labelled structures (A–E) within a rectangular cell boundary
plantCell.title: Plant cell structure
plantCell.description: A rectangular plant cell as seen under a light microscope. The cell wall is the thick, rigid outer boundary forming the rectangular shape. Just inside it, the cell membrane is a thin line pressed against the cell wall. The nucleus is a large, dark-stained oval structure positioned slightly off-centre, approximately 1/4 of the cell diameter. Several small, oval chloroplasts (darker green) are distributed around the edges of the cytoplasm, concentrated near the cell periphery. A large central vacuole occupies approximately 60% of the cell interior, shown as a pale region bounded by a visible tonoplast membrane. The cytoplasm is a thin layer between the vacuole and the cell membrane. Label markers A–E point from outside the cell to each structure with blue (#007AFF) leader lines.

parts.question: Label structures A–E on the plant cell diagram.

solutionSteps.text: A = Cell wall — the thick, rigid outer layer made of cellulose that provides structural support. Only plant cells have a cell wall.
B = Nucleus — the large structure that contains the cell's DNA and controls cell activities.
C = Chloroplast — the organelle that contains chlorophyll and is the site of photosynthesis. Only plant cells have chloroplasts.
D = Vacuole — the large, permanent central vacuole filled with cell sap. It keeps the cell turgid and stores substances.
E = Cell membrane — the thin, partially permeable membrane that controls what enters and leaves the cell. It sits just inside the cell wall.
A common mistake is confusing the cell wall with the cell membrane. The cell wall is the thick outer boundary; the cell membrane is a thin layer just inside it.

parts.question: The student's teacher shows her a photograph of a bacterium. Which of the following best describes the difference between a bacterial cell and the plant cell the student observed?

options.text: Bacterial cells are prokaryotic — they have no membrane-bound nucleus
Bacterial cells are eukaryotic — they have a nucleus surrounded by a membrane
Bacterial cells are larger than plant cells
Bacterial cells contain chloroplasts for photosynthesis

solutionSteps.text: The correct answer is A — bacterial cells are prokaryotic, meaning their DNA is free in the cytoplasm and is not enclosed in a membrane-bound nucleus.
Plant and animal cells are eukaryotic — they have a true nucleus with a nuclear membrane.
A common mistake is thinking 'prokaryotic' just means 'smaller'. The key difference is the absence of a membrane-bound nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles.

parts.question: Muscle cells in the legs of a long-distance runner contain many more mitochondria than skin cells. Which of the following best explains why?

options.text: Muscle cells need to store more glucose than skin cells
Muscle cells need to release more energy through aerobic respiration
Muscle cells need to carry out more photosynthesis
Muscle cells need to divide more rapidly by mitosis

solutionSteps.text: The correct answer is B — muscle cells need to release large amounts of energy through aerobic respiration to power muscle contraction.
Mitochondria are the organelles where aerobic respiration takes place, so cells with high energy demands contain more of them.
This is an application question: you need to connect the function of mitochondria (aerobic respiration → energy release) to the context of a cell with high energy requirements.

A common mistake is confusing mitochondria with chloroplasts. Chloroplasts carry out photosynthesis (found only in plant cells). Mitochondria carry out respiration (found in both plant and animal cells).

parts.question: The student observes a cell that is 0.05 mm long. Under the microscope, the image of the cell is 25 mm long. Calculate the magnification of the microscope.

methodMarks.description: Correct formula: magnification = image size ÷ actual size

solutionSteps.text: Use the magnification formula:
magnification = image size ÷ actual size
magnification = 25 mm ÷ 0.05 mm
magnification = ×500
The magnification is ×500. Note that magnification has no units — the mm cancel. A common mistake is forgetting to convert units before dividing, but here both values are already in mm.

parts.question: Which type of microscope would you need to see ribosomes inside a cell?

solutionSteps.text: The correct answer is C — an electron microscope.
Ribosomes are very small sub-cellular structures, approximately 20 nm in diameter.
Light microscopes have a maximum resolution of about 200 nm, so they cannot resolve structures as small as ribosomes.
Electron microscopes have much higher resolution (down to ~0.1 nm) and can clearly image ribosomes.
A common mistake is thinking higher magnification is enough. Resolution — the ability to distinguish two close points as separate — is the key factor, not magnification.
